title: useFetch with AbortController, dedup, and exponential-backoff retry
scenario_context: Internal admin tool fetches user lists across 30+ pages. Without dedup, tab focus refetch creates thundering herd. Without abort, navigating mid-fetch shows stale data in the new view. Without retry, transient 503s during deploys break the UI for the whole window.
verification_chain: Layer 6 (Client API): fetch wrapper threads AbortSignal through every call; HTTP errors throw typed FetchError with status/body
Layer 7 (State): hook returns discriminated union {status: 'idle'|'loading'|'success'|'error', data?, error?} — never both data and error simultaneously
Layer 8 (Component): consumer renders by status, not by truthy-check on data
Layer 9 (Interaction): unmount during fetch aborts request, no setState-after-unmount warning
Layer 10 (Observability): each retry logs attempt number and backoff delay; final failure logs full URL + correlation ID
anti_patterns_to_avoid: useEffect with [] deps that lies — url/options change but no refetch
Retrying on 4xx (client errors are not transient)
Calling setState in unmounted component because abort wasn't wired
Returning {data, loading, error} with all three potentially truthy
what_breaks_naive_approach: During deploy rollouts, the API returns 502 for ~8 seconds. Naive hook flips to error state, user reloads, gets the same error. A correct hook retries 3x with jitter (200ms, 600ms, 1.4s) and the user never sees it. Also: rapid filter changes on a search page send 8 in-flight requests; without abort, results render in arrival order — not request order — and the user sees results for the wrong filter.
reasoning: The hook is a state machine: idle → loading → (success | error) → loading (on refetch). Every transition must be valid. AbortController must be created per-call and stored in a ref so cleanup can abort. Retry only on 5xx and network errors (no response), never on 4xx — those are deterministic. Exponential backoff with full jitter prevents thundering herd when 100 clients retry simultaneously. Dedup via an in-memory Map keyed on URL+method+body avoids duplicate in-flight requests when two components mount with the same query. At 10k users, without dedup, a dashboard with 12 widgets calling the same /me endpoint makes 12 requests per page load.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: AppError hierarchy with status + code; errorHandler middleware serializes to {code, message, correlationId, fields?}
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: FetchError carries status + parsed ApiError; .retryable encapsulates retry policy
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Component switches on error.body?.code; falls back to generic 'Something went wrong — try again' for unknown codes
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: On error state, show Retry button that re-triggers useEffect by bumping a key; on 401, redirect to login
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: Each retry attempt logged with attempt number, delay, url; final failure includes correlationId so server logs are joinable

edge_cases_addressed: Component unmount during fetch — AbortController.abort() prevents setState
Two components mount with same URL same tick — dedup map serves one promise
429 with Retry-After header — counts as retryable
User toggles offline mid-fetch — network error treated as retryable
URL prop changes during retry — old controller aborted, new fetch begins
Server returns 200 with HTML (proxy interstitial) — JSON parse fails, treated as error
Stale closure on parse function — parse is captured per-effect-run
test_strategy: Unit: reducer transitions valid only. Hook: render with MSW returning 502 then 200, assert exactly 2 calls, state ends success. Abort: unmount mid-flight, assert no warning. Dedup: render two consumers same URL, assert one network call. E2E: throttle network in Playwright, verify retry logs.
observability_hooks: Datadog RUM: track fetch_attempt count, fetch_retry_total by url-pattern, fetch_duration histogram. Server: structured log per request with correlationId; alert on 5xx rate > 0.5% per route.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Discriminated union state instead of three booleans — makes invalid states unrepresentable. 2) Dedup at the fetcher layer, not the hook layer, so unrelated hooks benefit too. 3) Correlation ID round-trip so a user-reported bug ('it failed at 3:42pm') can be found in server logs in seconds.

title: useLocalStorage with cross-tab sync, JSON safety, and SSR guards
scenario_context: Settings panel persists theme/sidebar-collapsed/feature-toggles. User opens app in two tabs, changes theme in tab A — tab B must update. SSR hydration must not mismatch. Corrupted JSON from older app version must not crash the app.
verification_chain: Layer 7 (State): hook returns [value, setValue] with stable setter identity across renders
Layer 8 (Component): initial render returns defaultValue on server, real value after hydration (no mismatch warning)
Layer 9 (Interaction): writing in tab A fires storage event tab B receives within one tick
Layer 10 (Observability): parse failures logged with key + raw value (truncated)
anti_patterns_to_avoid: typeof window check inside render — causes hydration mismatch
JSON.parse without try/catch — single bad value blocks app
Storage event listener without cleanup — leaks across navigations
Serializing on every render — setter recreated, infinite effect loops
what_breaks_naive_approach: User had v1 of app that stored theme as 'dark' (string, no quotes). v2 stores as JSON ('"dark"'). On first load of v2, JSON.parse('dark') throws SyntaxError — entire React tree fails to mount. Correct hook treats parse failure as 'use default + log + heal on next write'.
reasoning: localStorage is synchronous, string-only, ~5MB. SSR has no window. Hydration must match server's initial render — so first client render must also return defaultValue, then useEffect reads localStorage and updates. Cross-tab sync via window.addEventListener('storage', ...) which fires in OTHER tabs (not the one that wrote). Setter must be stable (useCallback) so consumers can put it in dependency arrays. JSON.parse must be wrapped — old data, half-written data, or non-JSON data must degrade gracefully. At scale, lots of small writes during interaction (sidebar drag) — debounce write to avoid layout thrash from storage events.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: N/A for hook itself; settings PUT uses standard AppError hierarchy
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Parse failure → fallback to default + console.warn; quota exceeded → console.error + UI toast 'Settings could not be saved'
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Silent degradation for read (use default); toast on write failure with retry
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: On parse failure, next setValue heals the storage; on quota, suggest clearing old data
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: Sentry breadcrumb on parse failure with truncated raw value

edge_cases_addressed: SSR — typeof window undefined returns default
Tab A writes, tab B subscribes via storage event
localStorage disabled (Safari private mode) — try/catch around all access
Quota exceeded (5MB) — write fails gracefully
Schema migration — old v1 string parses fail, fall back to default
Same-tab writes — storage event doesn't fire, manual listener invocation
Schema changes between releases — Zod validation catches and resets
test_strategy: Unit: read with invalid JSON returns default. Cross-tab: mock storage event, assert subscribed components re-render. SSR: render to string with typeof window override, assert no mismatch. Quota: mock setItem to throw, assert error path.
observability_hooks: Sentry: breadcrumb on every parse/write failure. Metric: localStorage_quota_exceeded count per session.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) useSyncExternalStore for tear-free concurrent rendering — junior code uses useState + useEffect which can show inconsistent values across components. 2) Zod schema as runtime guard against schema drift between app versions. 3) Same-tab writes manually notify listeners because the storage event ONLY fires in other tabs.

title: Typed AppError hierarchy with code system and field-level errors
scenario_context: A SaaS form (project creation) can fail at: schema validation, name uniqueness, plan quota, billing past_due. Each failure has a distinct UX. Generic '500 Internal Server Error' loses every distinction. The client must map each failure to a specific field or banner.
verification_chain: Layer 3 (Service): each business rule throws a specific error subclass with code
Layer 4 (Controller): no try/catch — errors propagate to middleware; middleware serializes consistently
Layer 5 (API contract): error shape is part of the contract, schema'd in Zod
Layer 6 (Client API): fetcher parses errors with the same schema; throws typed FetchError
Layer 8 (Component): error.code switch routes to field-level or banner-level UX
Layer 10 (Observability): 4xx logged at warn (expected), 5xx at error (alerted on)
anti_patterns_to_avoid: throw new Error('quota exceeded') — string-matching the message in the client
res.status(500).json({ error: err.message }) — leaks stack to client
catch (e) { console.log(e) } at the controller — swallows + no structured log
Single error code 'BAD_REQUEST' for all validation failures — client can't map to fields
what_breaks_naive_approach: User on free plan tries to create 6th project. Server returns 500 with message 'Quota exceeded for plan'. Client shows generic 'Something went wrong'. User retries 5 times. Support gets ticket. Correct system: server throws QuotaExceededError with code QUOTA_EXCEEDED and upgrade URL in metadata; client shows 'You've reached your plan limit. Upgrade to Pro' with a CTA button.
reasoning: Errors are part of your API surface. They should be designed with the same care as success responses. A hierarchy AppError → {ValidationError, NotFoundError, ConflictError, AuthError, ForbiddenError, QuotaError, BillingError, RateLimitError, InternalError} gives both a class to catch and a code to discriminate. The HTTP status is metadata; the code is the contract. Field errors map naturally to react-hook-form's setError. Middleware decides log level — 4xx is expected user behavior (warn), 5xx is an unexpected condition (error + Sentry).

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: AppError subclasses for each business failure; ZodError normalized to ValidationError; unknown errors wrapped in InternalError (500)
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: FetchError.body.code switched into UX strategy: fields → inline errors; quota → upgrade banner with CTA; billing → 'Update payment method' banner; unknown → generic retry banner
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: VALIDATION/CONFLICT → field-level red text; QUOTA → 'You've reached your X limit on the Free plan' with Upgrade button; BILLING → 'Your subscription is past due' with Update Payment button
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Field errors: user edits and retries. Quota: navigate to /billing/upgrade. Billing: navigate to /billing/payment-method. Unknown: Retry button + correlation ID copy-to-clipboard
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: 4xx logged at warn with code+path+tenant. 5xx logged at error with full cause chain + correlationId. Sentry only captures 5xx. Metric: api_errors_total{code, path}

edge_cases_addressed: ZodError from validation middleware — auto-converted to ValidationError
Error has cause property — preserved through normalization
Non-Error thrown (e.g. throw 'foo') — wrapped in InternalError
Race: two requests both pass uniqueness check, second hits DB unique index → caught as ConflictError
Cross-locale: messages localized client-side from code, not from server's English message
Stack traces never leaked even in 500 responses
Correlation ID surface on client so users can paste in support tickets
test_strategy: Unit: each error class has correct status + code. Middleware: ZodError → ValidationError shape. Service: quota check throws QuotaError. Integration: POST /projects when over quota returns 402 with code QUOTA_EXCEEDED + meta.upgradeUrl. E2E: form shows upgrade banner with working CTA.
observability_hooks: Pino structured logger with err serializer. Sentry hooks only for status >= 500. Datadog metric api_errors_total tagged by code, path, tenant_plan. Dashboard: error rate by code per route, p95 latency on error path (errors should be FAST).
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Error codes are part of the API contract, schema'd in Zod, shared between server and client — not strings to be matched. 2) 4xx vs 5xx routes to different log levels — 4xx pages on-call for nothing, 5xx is real. 3) Meta payload lets QuotaError ship the upgrade URL — UX doesn't hardcode it, so plan changes don't require frontend deploys.

title: React Error Boundary with async errors, recovery actions, and Sentry
scenario_context: Production app has 50+ routes. A single component throwing in a render path white-screens the whole app. Async errors in useEffect bypass error boundaries entirely. Users see a blank page and bounce. Need: boundary per route, recovery actions, async error capture, structured Sentry context.
verification_chain: Layer 8 (Component): ErrorBoundary class component catches render-phase errors
Layer 8 (Component): useErrorHandler hook lets useEffect/async code throw INTO the boundary
Layer 9 (Interaction): boundary shows 'Reload section' / 'Go home' / 'Report issue' actions, not just text
Layer 10 (Observability): Sentry captures error with route, user, tenant, correlationId; breadcrumbs include last 20 user actions
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single top-level ErrorBoundary — one component error kills entire app
Boundary with no fallback UI — shows nothing
Boundary that doesn't reset on route change — stays broken even after navigation
Boundary fallback that itself can throw
try/catch in render — doesn't work for child components
what_breaks_naive_approach: User on /projects/123/insights page — a chart component throws because data has unexpected shape after a backend deploy. Without boundaries, entire SPA white-screens. User refreshes — same page, same crash. They leave. Correct: chart's containing card shows error fallback with 'Reload this section' and 'Report' button, rest of app keeps working. Sentry receives event with route, user ID, last data fetched.
reasoning: Error boundaries are class components — there is no hook equivalent in React 19 (componentDidCatch + getDerivedStateFromError). They catch errors in render, lifecycle, and constructors of descendants. They DO NOT catch event handlers, async code, server-rendered errors, or errors in the boundary itself. For async, the pattern is to setState({ error }) in the catch block then re-throw in render — the boundary catches the render-phase throw. The boundary takes a resetKey prop (e.g., route pathname) so navigation auto-recovers. Sentry must be initialized once and given user/tenant scope on auth state changes — then errors carry that context automatically.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: N/A — boundary catches client errors
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Render errors → caught by getDerivedStateFromError. Async errors → useAsyncError re-throws in render
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Section-level fallback with descriptive text + action buttons; never just 'Error'
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: 'Try again' resets boundary; 'Go to dashboard' navigates away from broken view; in dev, full stack visible; Sentry feedback widget available via 'Report issue'
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: Sentry.captureException with component stack, route, user context, breadcrumbs (last 20 actions). Client_error_total metric tagged by route.

edge_cases_addressed: Async error in useEffect — useAsyncError throws into boundary
Error during initial render of fallback itself — outer boundary catches
Route change while error displayed — resetKey changes, boundary clears
Hydration mismatch — caught and reported but app continues with client render
User-triggered error (button onClick) — try/catch in handler, manually report
Lazy-loaded chunk fails to load — separate ChunkLoadError handling with reload prompt
Boundary catches own error in componentDidCatch — guarded with try/catch around Sentry call
test_strategy: Unit: throw in child, assert fallback rendered. resetKey change clears error. useAsyncError throws Promise rejection into boundary. Integration: navigate after error → recovers. E2E: trigger error in real route, assert Sentry event in dashboard, assert app navigable.
observability_hooks: Sentry React integration with route tracking. Custom tag 'boundary_key' to identify which scope caught the error. Breadcrumbs include navigation events, API calls, form submissions. Performance: error rate by route, by component.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Per-route boundaries (resetKey = pathname) so errors don't persist after navigation — junior code has one boundary at the top. 2) useAsyncError pattern bridges the async gap React error boundaries don't natively cross. 3) Fallback UI gives users RECOVERY actions, not just a sad face — bounces drop dramatically.

title: Zod as single source of truth: DB schema → API contract → React form
scenario_context: User profile has 15 fields with different validation rules. Currently: Mongoose schema enforces some, Express validator enforces different rules, React form has its own. Three sources of truth drift apart over time. A required-field check passes one and fails another. Bug reports: 'I can save but can't load' / 'It says save then nothing changes'.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): Mongoose schema generated from Zod (or Zod validates before .save())
Layer 4 (Controller): same Zod schema parses req.body — no separate express-validator rules
Layer 5 (API contract): TypeScript types derived from Zod via z.infer
Layer 6 (Client API): same Zod schema imported in client to parse fetch responses
Layer 9 (Interaction): react-hook-form uses zodResolver with the same schema — client side matches server
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Mongoose schema with `required: true`, Zod schema without — drift
z.string() on server, z.string().min(1) on client — false positives
Duplicating types — type User in @shared/types AND in mongoose model AND in form
Server validation rejects but client thinks it accepted (no response parsing)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Phone field added with min length 10 on server. Forgot to update client form. User types 5 digits, submits, server returns 400. Client shows generic 'Save failed' (no field mapping). Worse: another field added on client validation but not server — XSS payload accepted. Single Zod schema: rule changes once, both sides know. Add field → both sides typecheck error if missed.
reasoning: Zod is the choke point. The shared package exports schemas. Server: parse req.body → if success, types are inferred and safe to pass to DAL; if failure, throw ValidationError with field map. Client: same schema in react-hook-form's zodResolver. Bonus: parse the API RESPONSE on the client too — if server adds a field, client's runtime check catches the drift before TypeScript would (because types are inferred from the schema, not from network bytes). For Mongoose, either generate the Mongoose schema from Zod (via zod-mongoose) or write the Mongoose schema with the minimum needed for indexing/storage and validate-before-save with Zod.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: validateBody middleware converts ZodError to ValidationError with field map
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: VALIDATION_FAILED with fields → setError per field via react-hook-form
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Each field shows its own error message from the schema's .min/.regex/.refine message
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: User fixes the highlighted field — Zod re-validates on change
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: Validation failures logged per field name to spot UX issues (high failure rate on 'phone' → improve placeholder/helper)

edge_cases_addressed: Server adds a field — client UserProfileSchema.parse on response throws, caught by error boundary
Client validates locally before submit (fast feedback) AND server re-validates (security)
Date fields use z.coerce.date so JSON strings parse correctly
Optional fields use .optional() not .nullable() to match form semantics
Refinement (timezone Intl check) runs identically on both sides
Schema evolution: add field as .optional().default() — backward compatible
Email lowercased via .transform on both sides — same input normalization
test_strategy: Property test: random valid User objects parse on both sides identically. Mutation test: invalidate one field, assert both server and client reject. Contract test: hit real server with fixture, parse response, assert no schema mismatch.
observability_hooks: Log every ValidationError with path map → dashboard shows top 10 most-rejected fields, lets you tune copy or UX.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Zod is the choke point — no parallel validation systems. 2) Parse the RESPONSE too, not just the request — catches backend drift before users see corrupted data. 3) Field-level error round-trip from server back into form via react-hook-form's setError — UX matches client-side validation seamlessly.

title: Tenant isolation enforced at DAL with AsyncLocalStorage context
scenario_context: Multi-tenant B2B SaaS. Each tenant's data must NEVER be visible to another. Currently controllers manually pass tenantId to services. One missing pass = cross-tenant data leak = company-ending incident. Need: tenant context propagated via AsyncLocalStorage, DAL refuses queries without it.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): every tenant-scoped collection has tenantId field with compound index (tenantId, ...)
Layer 2 (DAL): all queries are wrapped — automatically inject {tenantId: currentTenant()} from AsyncLocalStorage; throw if context missing
Layer 3 (Service): no service ever passes tenantId manually — gets it from context
Layer 4 (Controller): middleware sets tenant context from JWT/session before any handler runs
Layer 5 (API contract): tenant ID is NOT in URLs (avoid IDOR) — derived from auth
Layer 10 (Observability): every query logs current tenant ID; alert on queries with no tenant context
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Service receives tenantId as arg — easy to forget on a new endpoint
Tenant ID in URL like /tenants/:tenantId/projects — opens IDOR attacks
Raw model.find() in services — bypasses tenant filter
Trusting client to send X-Tenant-ID header — auth source is server-derived only
Single shared connection without row-level security or filter wrapping
what_breaks_naive_approach: Engineer adds new endpoint GET /reports. Forgets to filter by tenantId. In QA it returns all reports — engineer thinks 'reports are admin-only anyway'. In prod, tenant B's CEO opens the page, sees tenant A's revenue figures. Lawsuit. With AsyncLocalStorage + wrapped DAL, ANY query missing tenant context throws at runtime — caught in tests, can't reach prod.
reasoning: AsyncLocalStorage (node:async_hooks) is the right primitive: it propagates context through async operations without explicit threading. Middleware enters the store with the authenticated tenant, every subsequent await in the request keeps that context. The DAL is the choke point — instead of calling Mongoose models directly, services call DAL methods. DAL methods read currentTenant() from store and inject the filter. The DAL THROWS if context is missing (e.g., background job that forgot to seed context) rather than silently querying across tenants. Compound indexes (tenantId, createdAt) keep queries fast. Defense in depth: at the DB layer, consider Postgres RLS or Mongo views per tenant; even so, app-layer enforcement matters because it's where bugs originate.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: TENANT_CONTEXT_MISSING is a programming error (500); UnauthorizedError when JWT lacks tenant (401)
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: 401 → redirect to login; 500 with TENANT_CONTEXT_MISSING is a bug not user-facing
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Generic 401/403 messages — never reveal whether resource exists in another tenant
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Login on 401; for 403, no leak about other tenants
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: Every DAL call logs tenantId from context. Audit log for sensitive operations. Alert on TENANT_CONTEXT_MISSING — indicates a bug to fix.

edge_cases_addressed: Background job — runWithTenant() wrapper, throw if forgotten
Cross-tenant lookup attempt (passing :id from URL) — DAL adds tenantId filter, returns 404
Admin impersonation — context updated with impersonatedTenantId + audit event
WebSocket connections — tenant context attached per-connection, threaded into handlers
Tests — vitest setup helper runs each test in fake tenant context
Compound index missing — query plan alert; required index added in migration
Promise.all across services — each branch inherits context (ALS propagates correctly)
test_strategy: Unit: DAL.list without context throws. Integration: tenantA creates project, tenantB cannot read by ID. Property test: random pair of tenants, no method exposes one to the other. Static: lint rule forbids importing models directly in services — must go through DAL.
observability_hooks: Log enrichment: every request log includes tenantId. Datadog: requests_by_tenant. Alert: any DAL log without tenant_id field. Audit log table for admin impersonation start/end with all queries during impersonation tagged.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) AsyncLocalStorage removes the threading burden — context is implicit, can't be 'forgotten'. 2) DAL throws on missing context — defense beyond 'remember to filter'. 3) Tenant ID derived from auth, never from URL — eliminates entire IDOR class.

title: Stripe webhook handler with signature verification, idempotency, and async processing
scenario_context: Stripe webhooks update subscription state. Naive implementation processes inline → Stripe times out at 10s → retries → double-processed events → duplicate invoices, incorrect plan changes. Also: webhook secret not verified means anyone can POST fake events. Need: signature verify, fast 200, idempotent processing via job queue.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): processed_events collection with unique index on event.id; subscriptions collection with idempotency_key per state change
Layer 4 (Controller): raw body buffer required for signature verification (express.raw)
Layer 4 (Controller): respond 200 within 100ms after enqueueing
Layer 3 (Service): worker processes events; checks processed_events before applying changes
Layer 10 (Observability): every event logged with stripe_event_id, type, processing_duration; alert on processing lag > 5min
anti_patterns_to_avoid: app.use(express.json()) before webhook route — body parsed, signature breaks
Processing inline — Stripe retries on slow responses, creates duplicates
Trusting event.id without dedup — same retry processes twice
Using event payload without re-fetching from Stripe API — payload can be stale by the time you process
Returning 500 from webhook handler on bug — Stripe will retry forever
what_breaks_naive_approach: Stripe sends invoice.payment_succeeded. Server processes inline: extends subscription, grants entitlements, sends email. Operation takes 12s (Stripe's timeout is 10s). Stripe marks delivery failed and retries. Now: 2x extensions, 2x entitlements, 2x emails. User gets double-billed equivalent. Correct flow: verify signature → write to inbox table with unique id → respond 200 → worker dequeues → idempotent processing using event.id as natural key.
reasoning: Stripe webhooks are at-least-once. Your handler must be idempotent. Signature verification uses HMAC-SHA256 over the raw bytes — JSON parsing the body destroys it. The pattern: raw body middleware ONLY for this route, verify via stripe.webhooks.constructEvent. Save event to inbox with unique constraint on event.id — duplicate Stripe retries fail the insert (caught and ignored), original event already processed. Respond 200 immediately. Worker reads from inbox, claims with conditional update (status=pending → processing) so concurrent workers can't double-claim. Inside processing, re-fetch the object from Stripe (event payload may be stale by minutes) — use the API as source of truth. Mark processed_at; failures retry with backoff up to N times, then dead-letter for human triage.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Bad signature → 400 (do not retry). Inbox write fail (non-dup) → 500 (Stripe retries). Worker failure → keep status=failed, retry with backoff, dead-letter after 5 attempts
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: N/A for webhook; UI polls for eventual state
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: After checkout, 'Updating subscription' hint until status flips to active
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Dead-letter queue + alert; on-call investigates manually

edge_cases_addressed: Stripe retries event already processed — unique index on eventId blocks duplicate
Webhook receives event before checkout-success page loads (race) — UI polls for state
Two workers claim same event — conditional update only one wins
Stripe payload stale by minutes — re-fetch via API
Plan price ID renamed in Stripe dashboard — planFromPriceId guards with fallback + alert
Worker crashes mid-processing — heartbeat sweep marks 'processing > 5min' as pending again
Signature secret rotated — env var hot-reload via process.env on each request
test_strategy: Unit: planFromPriceId mapping. Replay: feed real Stripe event payload to handler twice, assert single processing. Signature: tamper bytes, assert 400. Integration: end-to-end with Stripe test mode + ngrok. Chaos: kill worker mid-process, assert sweep recovers.
observability_hooks: Pino logs: stripe_event_received, stripe_event_processed, stripe_event_failed (with type, eventId, durationMs). Dashboard: events/sec by type, p95 processing duration, dead-letter count. Alert: dead-letter > 0 pages on-call.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Inbox pattern: write fast, process async — Stripe never times out. 2) Re-fetch payload from Stripe API instead of trusting webhook body — eventual consistency on Stripe's side. 3) Conditional update for worker claim — no Redis lock needed, no double-processing.

title: RBAC + ABAC: roles for capability, attributes for resource ownership
scenario_context: App has admins, members, viewers. Members can edit their OWN projects, but not others'. Admins can edit any project in their tenant. Currently: ad-hoc if-checks scattered through controllers. New endpoints forget checks. UI shows edit buttons users can't use.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): roles stored per (userId, tenantId); resource ownership stored on resource (createdBy field)
Layer 3 (Service): authorize(action, resource) called before mutating; throws ForbiddenError
Layer 4 (Controller): no auth logic in controllers — trust service
Layer 5 (API contract): GET /me/permissions returns capability map for current user
Layer 8 (Component): <Can do="project:update" on={project}> hides UI for actions user can't perform
Layer 10 (Observability): every denied action logged with userId, action, resourceId
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Checking role in controller — service can be bypassed by other entry points (jobs, GraphQL)
Hiding button in UI but no server check — savvy user inspects DOM, fires request
Hardcoded role names in components — schema change breaks UI
One global isAdmin check — doesn't handle ownership cases
Permissions checked AFTER side effects — partial failures
what_breaks_naive_approach: Viewer role added. Frontend hides 'Delete' button via `if (role === 'admin')`. Backend uses isAuthenticated middleware only. Curious viewer crafts DELETE /api/projects/123 in DevTools — succeeds. Whole project gone. Correct: every mutation goes through authorize() in service. Permission map endpoint feeds UI. UI is convenience, server is enforcement.
reasoning: RBAC alone is insufficient because 'edit your own' is per-resource. Pure ABAC is overkill for simple capabilities. Hybrid: roles grant capabilities (project:create, project:update_any, project:update_own). Resource policies check the attribute (createdBy === userId). The authorize function takes (action, resource?) and decides. UI mirrors via /me/permissions returning a static capability map AND a function-style 'can' API for per-resource checks (rendered lazily). Server is always the source of truth — UI is purely for hiding affordances that wouldn't work anyway. Audit log captures every deny (potential probe) and every allow on sensitive actions (project:delete, member:remove).

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: ForbiddenError (403) for denies. Never leak whether resource exists — return 404 if user lacks read access.
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: 403 → 'You don't have permission'. UI should rarely show this since Can hides affordances; if it appears it indicates a stale permission cache.
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Hidden buttons rather than disabled + tooltip — disabled invites support questions
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: 'Request access' button for admin contact; refresh permissions on focus
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: auth_denied logged with full context; spike in denies for a user may indicate probing

edge_cases_addressed: User's role changes mid-session — /me/permissions refetched on focus
Resource ownership transferred — UI re-renders with new createdBy
Org owner can never demote self below admin — service-level invariant
Impersonation: admin acts as user — uses target's role for checks, audit log records actor
Cross-tenant resource lookup — tenant context throws before authorize even runs
Permission map drift between client and server — both import from @shared
SuperAdmin overrides logged separately — sensitive enough to require justification
test_strategy: Property test: for each (role, action, ownership) tuple, server and client agree. Integration: viewer hits DELETE → 403 + audit log entry. UI test: viewer never sees Delete button. Contract test: ROLE_GRANTS matrix tested as a fixture.
observability_hooks: auth_denied logs with route, action, userId, tenantId. Datadog: denies_by_role_action. Alert on sudden spike in denies for a user — possible compromised account or curious employee. Audit log: separate store with retention, append-only.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) UI permissions are a derived view of server policy — same source. 2) Authorize lives in services, not controllers — protects every entry point (REST, GraphQL, jobs). 3) Log allows for sensitive ops too, not just denies — gives forensics if a legitimate-looking action turns out to be account takeover.

title: Recharts revenue dashboard with empty/loading/error/single-point states + accessible tooltips
scenario_context: SaaS billing dashboard shows ARR trend across 90 days. Real data: 60% of customers have ZERO data points (new tenants), 5% have ONE point (just signed up), 30% sparse data with gaps, 5% dense. Naive chart renders nothing for empty, breaks visually for single point, and ignores gaps.
verification_chain: Layer 6 (Client API): API returns array of {date, arr} possibly empty
Layer 7 (State): hook returns explicit status: 'loading' | 'empty' | 'single' | 'ready' | 'error'
Layer 8 (Component): each status renders specific UI matching layout dimensions
Layer 8 (Component): chart has aria-label with summary; data points have role='img' with values
Layer 9 (Interaction): keyboard arrow keys navigate data points; tooltip is announced by screen reader
Layer 10 (Observability): empty-state views tracked separately from errors
anti_patterns_to_avoid: <ResponsiveContainer><LineChart data={data}/> — empty data shows blank rectangle
Generic 'No data' empty state — teaches nothing
Loading shown as spinner over chart — layout shift on resolve
Tooltip from Recharts default — no aria, screen readers see nothing
Color hardcoded blue — fails in dark mode and color-blind palettes
what_breaks_naive_approach: Empty tenant opens dashboard. Sees big blank rectangle with axis labels. Thinks app is broken. Bounces. Or: dense data loads slowly, chart pops into existence and shifts everything below it by 300px. Or: blind user can't access any of this data. Correct: skeleton matches chart dimensions, empty state teaches what to expect, single-point shows as labeled dot, dense data animates in.
reasoning: A chart is a component, and like all components it has loading/empty/error/success states. Loading needs to occupy chart dimensions to avoid CLS — use a skeleton with same aspect ratio. Empty state for charts is special — instead of 'No data' icon, show example chart shape with explanation 'Data appears here once you have customers'. Single point can't draw a line — show as scatter with the value labeled. Sparse data uses connectNulls={false} so gaps are honest. Accessibility: chart gets aria-label with summary ('Revenue trend: $12k to $45k over 90 days'), individual points become focusable with tabIndex, arrow keys traverse. Colors come from CSS variables that respect theme + are tested against color-blind simulators.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Revenue query failure → InternalError; cache stale → return stale-while-revalidate
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Network/parse failure → error state with Retry
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Error: 'We couldn't load the revenue trend. Retry?' (no stack)
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Retry button re-runs fetch; persistent failure suggests contacting support with correlationId

edge_cases_addressed: Zero data points — empty state CTA to set up plans
Single data point — number + explanation, no line
Sparse data (3 points over 90 days) — connectNulls={false} shows gaps
All-null after partial fetch — same as empty
Slow network — skeleton matches chart aspect ratio (no CLS)
Theme switch mid-render — CSS vars cascade, no flash
Window resize — ResponsiveContainer adjusts; aria-label stays correct
Color-blind users — palette tested against deuteranopia/protanopia
test_strategy: Storybook: render each state with fixtures. axe-core: each variant passes a11y. Visual regression: skeleton matches chart dimensions. Unit: state transitions cover all branches. E2E: blind tenant sees empty state with CTA.
observability_hooks: revenue_chart_state_total{state} so PM sees how many users hit empty vs ready. Daily report: % of dashboard loads by state.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Empty/single/loading/error/ready are FIVE distinct UIs, not one chart hiding behind a spinner. 2) Skeleton matches final layout dimensions — no CLS pushes content. 3) aria-label summarizes the chart so screen readers communicate the gist; tooltip is aria-live for keyboard nav.

title: Optimistic UI for star/unstar with rollback, dedup, and offline queueing
scenario_context: List of projects, each with a star button. Users star/unstar frequently. Network is slow on mobile. Naive impl shows spinner per click (feels broken). Optimistic toggle without rollback shows wrong state when server says no (already unstarred, rate limited, project deleted).
verification_chain: Layer 3 (Service): toggle is idempotent — sending star=true twice returns same state
Layer 4 (Controller): returns final canonical state, not just success
Layer 6 (Client API): mutation has stable request ID; dedup in-flight requests per resource
Layer 7 (State): optimistic state stored separately from server state; merges on response
Layer 8 (Component): star icon reflects optimistic state immediately, with subtle pending indicator
Layer 9 (Interaction): on failure, state reverts AND toast shows reason + undo
Layer 10 (Observability): track optimistic-revert rate (high = backend instability)
anti_patterns_to_avoid: setState then await fetch then setState — race: if user clicks twice fast, second update overwritten by first response
No rollback on failure — UI shows starred but server didn't accept
Spinner replacing the icon — every click feels broken on slow network
Allowing parallel requests for same resource — last write wins, sometimes the older one
what_breaks_naive_approach: User rapid-clicks star: star, unstar, star (3 clicks in 200ms). 3 requests fire. Network reorders responses. Final state on server: star (correct). UI shows: unstar (wrong — received second response last). User refreshes — UI flips back. They lose trust. Correct: dedup so only the latest intent is sent; or queue serially with optimistic state.
reasoning: Optimistic UI is a contract: trust the user's intent until the server contradicts. Implementation: maintain optimisticOverrides Map<resourceId, intent>. Render uses override if present, else server state. Mutation: apply override → fire request → on success, drop override (server's new state is now in cache via refetch or push); on failure, drop override AND show error with undo. For rapid clicks: track currentRequestId per resource. If new intent comes in while request in-flight, cancel old (AbortController), fire new. Or: queue and only fire latest. Idempotent server design (toggle to explicit value, not flip) prevents 'two clicks = unchanged' bugs.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: NOT_FOUND if project deleted; RATE_LIMITED if too many ops/sec
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: NOT_FOUND → toast 'Project no longer exists', remove from list; RATE_LIMITED → toast with backoff hint
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Failures show toast with action: 'Could not save. Retry' (button) or 'Project no longer exists' (dismiss)
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Retry button re-invokes toggle; refresh on persistent failures; offline → queued in IndexedDB
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: optimistic_revert_total{reason} so backend bugs are visible from frontend metrics

edge_cases_addressed: Rapid double-click — second toggle aborts first; only latest sent
Response arrives after another override has been set — requestId guard prevents stale override clear
Network error — toast with retry, override reverted
Project deleted between optimistic toggle and server response — error handled, removed from list
Tab refresh during pending mutation — server state unchanged because mutation didn't reach (or did, idempotently)
Server returns different value than requested (e.g., already starred, returns starred:true even when we toggled to false because of race) — server is truth, override cleared
Offline — request queued in IndexedDB, replayed on online event
test_strategy: Unit: reducer + override map transitions. Hook: rapid clicks send one request. Mock server with delay: response order doesn't matter. Failure: optimistic state reverts AND toast shown. Storybook: pending visual state. E2E: throttle network, star/unstar feels instant; kill backend, see revert + toast.
observability_hooks: client_optimistic_revert_total{reason} (server_error|network|aborted). Alert if revert rate > 1% — points to backend instability. Latency: star_mutation_duration_seconds p95 visible.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Idempotent set-to-value API design — two 'star' clicks don't toggle off. 2) requestId guard against late responses overwriting newer intents. 3) Offline queue gives the optimistic feel real durability — mobile users on subways can still interact.

title: useDebounce with leading/trailing edge control and cancellation
scenario_context: Search input fires API call on every keystroke. 10 keystrokes = 10 requests. Backend gets hammered, latest results race with earlier ones, user sees flicker. Need debounce that supports leading edge (immediate first call), trailing edge (final call after pause), maxWait (forced flush), and explicit cancel on unmount.
verification_chain: Layer 7 (State): debounced value separate from immediate value — components can render typed state while waiting
Layer 8 (Component): consumers use debounced value in API calls only
Layer 9 (Interaction): typing then immediate submit triggers flush (don't lose final input)
Layer 10 (Observability): debounce_flush_total to measure savings
anti_patterns_to_avoid: setTimeout in useEffect without cleanup — stacks timers on rerenders
Debouncing the API call instead of the value — losing closure over latest state
No maxWait — fast typists never trigger search
Not cancelling on unmount — setState on unmounted component
what_breaks_naive_approach: User types 'react query' fast. 12 keystrokes in 1.2s with 300ms debounce = no calls fire because each keystroke resets the timer. User sees nothing happening, types more, eventually pauses, finally sees result. Add maxWait=1000 — searches start within 1s of first keystroke, then update every 1s.
reasoning: Debouncing the VALUE not the callback gives consumers the right semantics. Implementation: useRef holds the latest value + timestamp. setTimeout schedules update. Each new value resets the timer. maxWait tracks 'first call since last flush' and forces a flush if exceeded. Leading edge: call immediately if no timer pending. Trailing edge: call after delay. Both can coexist. Cancel via cleanup. Flush method exposes the pending value before it would naturally fire (useful for form submit).
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/search.ts\nimport { z } from 'zod';\nexport const SearchQuerySchema = z.object({ q: z.string().min(1).max(500), cursor: z.string().optional() });

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Validation error on empty q → field error
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Empty query short-circuits API call
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Inline 'Enter at least 1 character'
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: User types more

edge_cases_addressed: Fast typist exceeding delay forever — maxWait forces flush
Form submit before debounce fires — flush() captures latest value
Unmount with pending timer — cleanup cancels
Same value set twice — Object.is short-circuits
Component re-renders rapidly — refs avoid stale closures
Delay 0 — synchronous-ish behavior
Switching between focused inputs — each instance independent
test_strategy: Vitest fake timers: assert exactly one invoke after burst. maxWait test: 100ms intervals exceeding 1s force flush. Cancel test: unmount cancels timer, no setState warning.
observability_hooks: debounce_flush_total counter exposed via prom-client. Histogram of input-to-flush latency.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Debounces the VALUE not the callback — consumers freely use value in effects/conditions without stale closures. 2) maxWait prevents debounce starvation under sustained input. 3) Explicit flush API integrates with form-submit and blur events.

title: useIntersectionObserver as factory: lazy images, infinite scroll, view tracking
scenario_context: Multiple features need viewport detection: lazy-load images, infinite-scroll feed pages, track impression analytics. Naive: each component creates its own IntersectionObserver. With 500 list items, that's 500 observers — measurable performance hit. Need: shared observer instance with per-element callbacks.
verification_chain: Layer 8 (Component): hook returns ref to attach + isIntersecting bool
Layer 8 (Component): consumers can opt into single-fire (impression tracking) or continuous (lazy-load reset)
Layer 9 (Interaction): scroll triggers update without blocking main thread
Layer 10 (Observability): impression_total tracked once per element per session
anti_patterns_to_avoid: new IntersectionObserver per component — too many observers
Forgetting to disconnect on unmount — leaks DOM nodes
Setting state every frame on intersection ratio — re-render storm
Polyfill without feature detection — wasted bytes
what_breaks_naive_approach: Infinite feed with 1000 items × image lazy + impression tracking = 2000 observers. Chrome devtools shows scroll perf < 30fps. Hook bundles into one observer per (root, threshold) combo via singleton map — 2000 elements share 2-4 observers. Scroll perf back to 60fps.
reasoning: IntersectionObserver is itself efficient, but each instance has memory + per-frame work. Group observations by (root, threshold, rootMargin) signature into shared observers. Hook attaches via ref callback, registers with shared observer keyed on signature, unregisters on unmount. Single-fire variant disconnects immediately on first intersect — perfect for impression tracking that shouldn't double-count. SSR-safe by skipping in absence of IntersectionObserver (e.g., old browsers).

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Batched ingestion returns 202 even if some rejected
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Failures swallowed (analytics non-critical) with sentry breadcrumb
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: None
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Retry via beacon on visibilitychange=hidden
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: client_observer_count gauge — alert if creeps up suggests leak

edge_cases_addressed: Server-side rendering — no IntersectionObserver, hook no-ops
Element reused with different observation opts — properly unregistered
Multiple consumers same options — one shared observer
Fast scroll past element — once:true still fires
Element removed from DOM — bucket cleanup on unmount
Browser without API — feature detect
Many elements (1000+) — shared observer keeps perf
test_strategy: jsdom mock IntersectionObserver. Render N components, assert one observer per unique options. once:true unobserves on first hit. Unmount cleans target.
observability_hooks: Datadog: client_intersection_observers gauge. Impression batch ingest rate to compare against client emit rate (delta = drop).
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Singleton map by signature — naive code makes one observer per component, this scales linearly. 2) Once-mode for impression tracking prevents double-counting on visibility flicker. 3) SSR-safe feature detection — junior code throws on import in Node.

title: Centralized error correlation IDs across logs, sentry, and HTTP responses
scenario_context: User reports 'It failed at 3:42pm Tuesday'. Engineers grep logs by approximate timestamp + user ID — frequently mismatched, sometimes wrong tenant's logs. Need: correlation ID injected at request entry, propagated through every log line, included in response (success and error), surfaced to user for support pasting.
verification_chain: Layer 4 (Controller): middleware assigns correlationId from header or new UUID; sets on res header
Layer 4 (Controller): every log line in request scope includes correlationId via async-local logger
Layer 5 (API contract): all responses (200 and error) include x-correlation-id header
Layer 6 (Client API): fetcher captures correlation header into FetchError
Layer 8 (Component): error UI shows correlationId with copy-to-clipboard
Layer 10 (Observability): Sentry tags events with correlationId; logs are queryable by it
anti_patterns_to_avoid: correlationId on req but logger doesn't auto-include — must remember to pass everywhere
Different IDs per microservice — can't trace cross-service
Generating new ID per log line — defeats the purpose
Showing user the internal stack instead of correlationId
what_breaks_naive_approach: Bug report: 'Saving the form failed.' Engineer asks for screenshot, time, browser, user. Hour later, finds it. With correlationId UI, user pastes 'corr-id: abc-123' → engineer does `grep abc-123 logs` → finds full trace in 5 seconds.
reasoning: AsyncLocalStorage holds the correlationId per request scope. A wrapped logger (pino child) is created at middleware time with the correlationId bound. Anywhere in the request lifecycle that imports the request logger gets contextual logs without parameter threading. Header-pass-through: if upstream provided x-correlation-id, reuse (cross-service tracing); else generate. Response always includes the header. Sentry's scope.setTag('correlationId', id) makes errors searchable in Sentry by the same ID.
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/correlation.ts\nimport { z } from 'zod';\nexport const CorrelationIdSchema = z.string().uuid();

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: errorHandler middleware reads correlationId from context, includes in error response
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: FetchError carries the correlationId for any UX surfacing
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Contact support with this ID' enables fast triage
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: User clicks pill → ID copied → pastes in support ticket
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: correlationId on every log line; queryable in Datadog/Splunk/CloudWatch

edge_cases_addressed: Client-provided x-correlation-id reused — cross-service tracing
Background job — runWithContext seeded with new ID
WebSocket — context per connection
Worker thread — context propagated via explicit pass
Long-running stream — single correlationId for whole stream
Sentry scope isolation — correlationId tag per event, not shared across requests
test_strategy: Integration: trigger error, capture correlation header, grep logs for ID, assert full trace present. Unit: middleware sets context. Snapshot: error response shape includes correlationId.
observability_hooks: Pino logs include correlationId field — Datadog facet on it. Sentry tag correlationId. Trace ID linked to Datadog APM.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) AsyncLocalStorage means logger gets correlationId automatically — no parameter threading. 2) User-facing pill turns support tickets from detective work into grep-and-find. 3) Header pass-through enables cross-service tracing without OpenTelemetry.

title: Network-aware retry with offline queue and exponential backoff with jitter
scenario_context: Mobile users on flaky connections. Save action fails on subway. Naive: 'Save failed, please try again' — user repeats action, gets the same error. Need: detect offline, queue mutations, retry on online, show queue status, prevent duplicates.
verification_chain: Layer 6 (Client API): mutations marked idempotent or not — only idempotent queue offline
Layer 7 (State): queue stored in IndexedDB so survives reload
Layer 9 (Interaction): UI shows 'Saved offline, will retry' instead of error
Layer 9 (Interaction): online event drains queue, surfaces successes/failures
Layer 10 (Observability): offline_queue_size gauge; offline_replay_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Retry forever without backoff — thunders the server on recovery
Queue non-idempotent operations — POSTs without unique key get duplicated
Storing queue in memory — lost on refresh
No user feedback about queue — silent failure
Replaying queue with stale auth — all fail
what_breaks_naive_approach: User on flight wifi writes a doc. Save fails. They try 5 times. Each generates a duplicate doc when wifi briefly recovers. They land with 6 copies. Correct: client-generated idempotency key on each save → server dedupes → user sees one doc.
reasoning: navigator.onLine + online/offline events are starting points, but unreliable (says online when connected to wifi without internet). Layer detection: assume online until a request fails with network error, then probe. Queue idempotent mutations to IndexedDB with idempotency key, request body, target URL, attempts. Background drain on online event with exponential backoff + jitter. Server checks idempotency key against a window (24h) — duplicate keys return original response. UI shows queue indicator ('3 changes pending') and per-item status. Auth tokens may expire while queued — refresh on drain.
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/idempotency.ts\nimport { z } from 'zod';\nexport const IdempotencyKeySchema = z.string().uuid();

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: IDEMPOTENCY_KEY_REUSED (409) when same key with different payload
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Idempotency replay returns cached response — appears as instant retry success
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Saved offline, will retry when connected' instead of 'Save failed'
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Auto-drain on online; manual 'Retry now' button on indicator

edge_cases_addressed: Auth token expires while queued — refresh-token flow before drain
Same payload retried — idempotency key matches → cached response returned
Different payload, same key (bug) — server returns 409 → drop and surface to user
Browser closed mid-queue — IndexedDB persists; resumes next session
Drainer running when network drops mid-drain — current request fails, retry on next online
User edits same record twice while offline — most recent payload wins via timestamp
Server permanently 4xx (validation) — don't retry forever, drop and surface
test_strategy: Vitest fake IDB + fake fetch. Enqueue 10, simulate online, assert all drain. Mock 429s, assert backoff schedule. Idempotency: enqueue + drain twice, assert one server call.
observability_hooks: client_offline_queue_size gauge. dropped_after_attempts_total{reason}. Server: idempotency_hit_rate (high = client retries working as designed).
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) IndexedDB queue survives reloads — junior code uses memory and loses everything. 2) Idempotency key prevents duplicates server-side — client retries are safe. 3) UX surfaces queue state — users feel in control rather than confused.

title: Branded types for IDs prevent passing UserId where TenantId expected
scenario_context: Codebase has UserId, TenantId, ProjectId, OrgId. All are UUIDs typed as string. Refactor swaps argument order in updateProjectMember(projectId, userId) → updateProjectMember(userId, projectId). Tests pass (UUIDs are valid either way). Production: 'user X granted access to project that isn't theirs'.
verification_chain: Layer 5 (API contract): Zod schemas use branded types
Layer 6 (Client API): API client signatures use branded types
Layer 7 (State): state shape preserves branding
Layer 3 (Service): service signatures prevent mismatched IDs at compile time
Layer 10 (Observability): logs include id with brand prefix when serialized for clarity
anti_patterns_to_avoid: type UserId = string — does nothing
interface User { id: string } and using user.id as both UserId and ProjectId
Casting between brands with `as` — defeats the purpose
Stringifying brands for logs without preserving meaning
what_breaks_naive_approach: Function grantAccess(projectId: string, userId: string) → swap call site to grantAccess(userId, projectId). TS happy. Tests pass. Prod: data corrupted. With brands, the call site fails to compile.
reasoning: Branded (or nominal) types let TS distinguish two strings at the type level. Zod has .brand() — z.string().uuid().brand<'UserId'>(). Helper constructors validate + brand. The runtime cost is zero (brand is phantom). Throughout the stack, types use UserId/TenantId/ProjectId. The DB returns plain strings; the DAL brands them on the way out. The API serializer outputs plain strings; the client parses with branded schemas. End result: passing UserId where ProjectId expected is a compile error.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: ID schema parse failure → ValidationError with which ID failed
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: asUserId throws ZodError if not a UUID — caught at the call site
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Invalid user identifier' — rarely reaches users since IDs come from server
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Reload page if IDs are corrupt
error_handling_strategy.telemetry: id_brand_mismatch_total — alert if non-zero (indicates a bug)

edge_cases_addressed: URL params are strings — must parse-and-brand at the boundary
JSON parse turns IDs back into strings — Zod schemas re-brand
Mongo _id is ObjectId — toString + brand at DAL boundary
SQL bigint IDs — same pattern with z.coerce.bigint().brand()
Brand assertion on test fixtures via factories that produce branded values
Logs serialize branded IDs as their string value (no brand in JSON)
Imports across packages share the brand definition via @shared
test_strategy: Type-only test (tsd) asserting grantAccess(userId, projectId) is an error. Runtime: asUserId rejects non-UUID. Integration: end-to-end roundtrip preserves type narrowing.
observability_hooks: Log id fields with prefix like user:<uuid>, project:<uuid> for human-readability without affecting type system.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Brands are free at runtime, expensive only at write time — high return on small investment. 2) Boundary-only validation: parse at API/DB edges, trust the type elsewhere. 3) Lint rule + tsd test guarantees brands aren't silently weakened via `as` casts.

title: Multi-step onboarding wizard with persistent state across reloads + reentrancy
scenario_context: 6-step onboarding: workspace name → invite team → choose plan → connect integration → import data → tour. Users abandon mid-flow. They come back days later expecting to resume. Naive: state in React, lost on close. Need: server-side persistence, resumability, ability to skip optional steps, idempotent step submissions.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): onboarding_progress table with stepId, status, completedAt, payload
Layer 3 (Service): nextStep() returns the next incomplete required step
Layer 4 (Controller): POST /onboarding/steps/:id idempotent (re-submit same data = no-op)
Layer 5 (API contract): GET /onboarding returns full state including which steps are complete
Layer 7 (State): client mirrors server state; server is truth
Layer 8 (Component): wizard reads server state on mount, jumps to correct step
Layer 9 (Interaction): browser refresh, switch device, all resume correctly
Layer 10 (Observability): step funnel metrics (drop-off per step)
anti_patterns_to_avoid: State in localStorage only — switching devices loses it
Steps in URL path without server validation — user navigates past prereqs
Form submission triggers irreversible side effects per step — refresh duplicates
Auto-completing all skippable steps — user can't come back to opt in
what_breaks_naive_approach: User completes 4 of 6 steps on laptop. Opens iPhone, logged in same account, sees step 1 again. Re-fills workspace name → backend creates duplicate workspace. Now they have two workspaces with same name + half their team in each. Correct: server tracks progress per user, GET on mount jumps to step 5.
reasoning: Onboarding is a state machine with persistent state. Each step is a record. POST submits payload, server validates + marks complete. GET returns canonical state. Required vs optional steps tracked in step config. Skippable steps explicit user action ('Skip for now'). Idempotency by submitting full payload — server checks complete-status before mutating; if already complete, returns prior result without re-executing side effects (no duplicate invitations). The wizard reads state on every navigation to detect concurrent changes (user completes step on another device). Funnel tracking: emit event per step start/complete/skip.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Cannot skip required step → 403; validation per step schema
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Validation errors mapped to fields; 403 → button hidden
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Inline per step; banner on system errors
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Step can be re-submitted; previously completed steps no-op

edge_cases_addressed: User completes step on device A then opens device B — focus event refetches state
Browser refresh mid-form — server has no record, user resumes that step
Required step skipped via API tampering — server rejects
Network failure on submit — UI shows retry; idempotency means retry safe
User invites the same email twice — idempotency at invite service
Plan step requires payment — Stripe checkout interrupts; resume on return
All optional skipped — flow still completes via tour
test_strategy: Integration: submit step → GET shows next. Idempotency: submit twice → one workspace. Cross-device: simulate two sessions, completion in one reflects in the other after refocus.
observability_hooks: funnel_step_started_total{step}, funnel_step_completed_total{step}, funnel_drop_off_total{step}. Per-tenant funnel dashboard for PM.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Server-of-truth onboarding state — survives device switches, refreshes, support handovers. 2) Idempotent step submissions — refreshes don't duplicate side effects. 3) Funnel metrics built in — PMs see drop-off without instrumenting later.

title: Recharts streaming line chart for live metrics with no flicker on data update
scenario_context: Real-time CPU usage chart updating every 1s. Naive: replace data array → full re-render flashes. Need: smooth scroll-shift, capped buffer, animation on initial render only, throttled to viewport visibility.
verification_chain: Layer 6 (Client API): SSE or WebSocket pushes metric ticks
Layer 7 (State): ring buffer caps at N points, drops oldest
Layer 8 (Component): isAnimationActive=false on updates after first render
Layer 8 (Component): chart container has fixed height to avoid CLS
Layer 9 (Interaction): pause/resume button stops appending
Layer 10 (Observability): tick_drop_total if buffer overrun
anti_patterns_to_avoid: data={[...data, newPoint]} unbounded — memory grows forever
Re-creating data array each tick — React reconciles unnecessarily
Animations on every update — janky 1Hz pop-in
Polling instead of pushing — wasted requests
Updating in background tab — wasted CPU
what_breaks_naive_approach: Page open for 4 hours. Buffer holds 14400 points. Re-render takes 200ms per tick. Tab CPU at 100%. Correct: cap at 300 points (5 min view), drop oldest. Pause on tab hidden.
reasoning: Recharts animates on data change by default. For streaming, turn animation off entirely after first render — visual smoothness comes from constant flow, not per-update animation. Ring buffer keeps memory bounded. requestAnimationFrame batching aggregates multiple ticks into one render if they arrive close together. visibilitychange event pauses updates when tab is hidden, resumes on focus. WebSocket reconnect on disconnect with exponential backoff.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: SSE client auto-reconnects on stream errors
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Backoff with cap; show 'reconnecting' indicator
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Subtle yellow dot when disconnected
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Auto-reconnect; manual reload

edge_cases_addressed: Tab hidden — pause ingestion, save CPU
Connection drops — exponential backoff reconnect
Buffer overrun (server burst) — slice from end
Server sends malformed JSON — Zod safeParse skips bad tick
Initial render on slow server — chart shows skeleton
Memory: ring buffer caps
Animation disabled — no per-tick pop
test_strategy: Mock EventSource emits 1000 ticks/sec; assert buffer stays at maxPoints. Hidden tab pauses ingestion. Disconnect triggers reconnect.
observability_hooks: sse_connection_lifetime histogram, tick_processing_lag p95.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) RAF batching coalesces bursts into one render. 2) Ring buffer prevents memory leak over hours. 3) Pause on tab hidden saves real user CPU.

title: Force-directed graph in Visx with collision detection and constrained drag
scenario_context: Org chart visualization with 200 employees. Need: nodes don't overlap, drag to reposition with parent following, zoom/pan with bounded viewport, click to focus subtree. Recharts can't do this.
verification_chain: Layer 6 (Client API): /org/graph returns nodes + edges
Layer 7 (State): simulation state held in ref (not React state) for performance
Layer 8 (Component): renders SVG with one <g> per node; uses transform for position
Layer 9 (Interaction): drag updates position; release re-runs simulation around dragged node
Layer 10 (Observability): graph_render_fps if FPS drops below threshold
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Storing every tick in React state — re-render per tick at 60Hz
Forgetting to stop simulation on unmount — leaks CPU
Drag handlers via React event — pointer capture lost on fast moves
Unbounded zoom — content lost off-screen
what_breaks_naive_approach: 200 nodes × React state per tick × no virtualization = 5 FPS. Drag is jittery. With visx + d3-force in ref, simulation runs at 60 FPS, React rerender once on simulation end.
reasoning: d3-force runs a simulation; for performance, mutate positions outside React, then render via SVG transforms. Use refs for the simulation and node array. requestAnimationFrame loop updates positions. Drag uses pointer events with setPointerCapture so fast moves don't lose tracking. Zoom/pan via d3-zoom with translateExtent bounded to graph bounding box. Click focus animates camera to subtree centroid.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: GraphSchema.parse rejects malformed
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Error boundary catches; falls back to list view
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Graph unavailable, showing list'
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Retry button

edge_cases_addressed: Disconnected components — multiple force centers
Self-loops — filtered out
Very large graphs (1000+ nodes) — virtualization or downsampling
Drag past viewport — pan automatically
Re-render when graph changes — simulation restart
Reduced motion — simulation stopped, fixed layout
Pointer capture lost on iOS — fallback to mouse events
test_strategy: Storybook with N=10, 100, 1000 nodes. Performance budget: 60fps with N=200. Drag in Playwright.
observability_hooks: client_graph_fps gauge; client_graph_simulation_duration histogram
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Position mutation outside React — orders of magnitude faster. 2) Pointer events with capture handle fast drag reliably across devices. 3) Bounded zoom prevents users from losing the graph off-screen.

title: Chart.js large dataset with decimation and zoom for 100k points
scenario_context: Trading-style price chart with 100k tick points. Recharts SVG can't render this many. Chart.js canvas can, but needs decimation to keep interaction snappy. Zoom into ranges reveals more detail.
verification_chain: Layer 6 (Client API): API returns paginated ranges by timestamp
Layer 7 (State): hold full dataset in ref; decimation applied to viewport
Layer 8 (Component): chart.js options enable decimation plugin (LTTB algorithm)
Layer 9 (Interaction): zoom triggers fetch of higher-resolution data for range
Layer 10 (Observability): chart_render_ms histogram
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Loading all 100k points and letting Chart.js do everything — laggy hover
No decimation — wasted pixels (multiple points per pixel)
Re-creating Chart on every data update — leak; need .update('none')
Default tooltips with 100k points — show wrong nearest
Not destroying chart on unmount — canvas leak
what_breaks_naive_approach: 100k points × no decimation = first paint 4s, hover latency 800ms. With LTTB to 2000 visible points, paint < 200ms, hover instant. On zoom, fetch hi-res data for that range.
reasoning: Chart.js's built-in decimation plugin with LTTB algorithm preserves the visual shape with ~2k points. For multi-resolution, fetch monthly aggregates initially, daily on zoom-in, hourly deeper. Use chartjs-plugin-zoom with onZoomComplete to trigger data fetch. Chart.update('none') for data updates without animation. Destroy chart on unmount to release WebGL/canvas. requestAnimationFrame the resize handler.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Range too large → 400 with code RANGE_TOO_LARGE
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: On zoom fetch failure, keep current data + toast
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Couldn't load detail — showing summary'
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Retry zoom

edge_cases_addressed: 100k+ points — decimation
Very deep zoom past data resolution — server caps
Rapid zoom changes — debounce fetch, abort prior
Theme switch — chart options.colors reactive (re-create or option-update)
Unmount mid-fetch — abort
Tooltip on dense data — index mode picks nearest x
WebGL context loss — re-create on contextlost event
test_strategy: Render 100k points, measure FCP < 200ms. Zoom in, assert fetch URL has correct resolution. Destroy on unmount, no memory growth.
observability_hooks: client_chart_render_ms histogram. server tick query duration p95.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Decimation at the chart layer + multi-resolution fetches — both client and server contribute. 2) Imperative chart needs explicit destroy on unmount. 3) onZoomComplete callback bridges chart events to React data fetching.

title: ECharts cross-chart filtering dashboard with brushing and theme integration
scenario_context: Executive dashboard: revenue by region (map), revenue by product (bar), revenue over time (line). Selecting a region filters the others. Selecting time range filters the others. Naive: each chart owns state, manual prop drilling. Need: shared filter context + ECharts dispatchAction for brush sync.
verification_chain: Layer 7 (State): FilterContext holds {region?, product?, dateRange?}
Layer 8 (Component): each chart reads filter and emits events
Layer 8 (Component): theme synced via echarts.registerTheme on dark/light switch
Layer 9 (Interaction): brushing a chart updates context which re-fetches all
Layer 10 (Observability): dashboard_filter_applied_total{type}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Local state per chart — filters don't propagate
Recreating ECharts instance per render — flicker + leak
Hardcoded colors — breaks dark mode
Synchronous re-fetch on every brush tick — server overwhelmed
what_breaks_naive_approach: Brush 'Q3 2025' on line chart — bar and map still show full year. User confused. Add filter context, brush dispatches setFilter, all charts re-render with shared state.
reasoning: FilterContext + reducer holds current filters. Each chart subscribes, refetches data when relevant filter changes. ECharts instance kept in ref; setOption(...) with notMerge:false for updates. Brushing via echarts.connect or manual brushSelected event handler → dispatch filter action. Theme: register both 'app-light' and 'app-dark' themes, swap via ref on theme change. Debounce brush selection to avoid overwhelming backend.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Filters validated; bad filter → 400
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: On chart fetch failure, show inline error per chart
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Couldn't load this chart — Reset filters?'
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Reset filters button

edge_cases_addressed: No data after filter — empty state per chart
Brush selects empty range — chart shows empty
Theme switch mid-render — re-init chart with new theme
Rapid filter changes — debounce fetch + abort prior
Map data missing for region — fall back to '?' marker
Mobile — charts stack with same filter UX
Export filtered view — share URL with filter query params
test_strategy: Brushing line updates URL + bar + map. Theme toggle re-renders all. Filters → reset returns full data.
observability_hooks: Filter usage tracked → PM sees which filters matter. Per-chart fetch latency.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Shared filter context with dispatcher — clean, debuggable, URL-shareable. 2) ECharts instance in ref, dispose on unmount — junior code leaks. 3) Theme as a registered ECharts theme — single source of truth across charts.

title: Chart with color-blind safe palette, dark mode, and screen-reader narration
scenario_context: Compliance dashboard used by enterprise customers with WCAG AAA requirements. Charts must be accessible to color-blind users, work in dark mode, and have screen-reader narration of data.
verification_chain: Layer 8 (Component): palette from a tested color-blind safe set (e.g., Okabe-Ito)
Layer 8 (Component): chart container has role='img' with aria-label and full text alternative
Layer 8 (Component): theme variables drive all colors
Layer 9 (Interaction): keyboard nav between data series; arrow keys cycle points
Layer 9 (Interaction): screen reader announces value on focus
Layer 10 (Observability): axe a11y violations tracked
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Red/green stoplight — invisible to red-green color-blind users
Hardcoded #fff backgrounds — invisible in dark mode
aria-label='Chart' — screen reader gets nothing useful
No keyboard focus on data points
Distinguishing series only by color (not also by line style/marker)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Red/green dual-axis chart. Color-blind exec can't tell which is which. Or: dark mode background but light chart → unreadable.
reasoning: Use Okabe-Ito palette (8 colors verified for deuteranopia/protanopia/tritanopia). Encode series identity redundantly: color + line style + marker shape (line dashed vs solid, circles vs squares). All colors via CSS custom properties so dark mode swaps automatically. Provide a table alternative below the chart for screen readers, OR make data points focusable with aria-label per point. Test with axe-core in CI.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Series data fetch — standard
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Show fallback table only
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Chart unavailable, see table below'
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Refresh

edge_cases_addressed: Color-blind users — palette + line styles
Screen reader — figcaption + table alternative
Dark mode — CSS vars
Print — chart hidden, table shown via @media print
High-contrast OS — forced-colors media query
Mobile — table scrollable
Reduced motion — no animations
test_strategy: axe-core CI fails build on violations. Storybook with color-blind simulator. Manual NVDA/VoiceOver walkthroughs.
observability_hooks: a11y violation events tracked in Sentry; per-route violation count.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Redundant encoding (color + style) makes charts robust to color limitations. 2) Table alternative is shipped, not a TODO. 3) CSS variables drive theme — junior code hardcodes #fff.

title: Adding 'archived' status to projects: DB → DAL → service → API → form → list filter
scenario_context: Product asks: 'add archive button to projects'. Junior PR touches DB and one button. QA misses: list still shows archived projects, search hits archived, exports include them, invitations to archived still send. Need: full-chain change with default filter, opt-in inclusion, type-safe everywhere.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): add archivedAt: Date | null with index on (tenantId, archivedAt)
Layer 2 (DAL): list query filters out archived by default; takes includeArchived flag
Layer 3 (Service): archive operation idempotent; cannot archive already-archived; un-archive separate op
Layer 4 (Controller): POST /projects/:id/archive; PATCH /projects supports archived filter
Layer 5 (API contract): Project schema adds archivedAt as nullable date
Layer 6 (Client API): projectsApi.list takes { includeArchived?: boolean }
Layer 7 (State): list state has 'show archived' toggle
Layer 8 (Component): archived items visually distinct (gray, badge)
Layer 9 (Interaction): archive button confirms; un-archive in archived view
Layer 10 (Observability): archive_total, unarchive_total counters
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Boolean archived field — loses timestamp
Soft-delete via deletedAt — confuses 'archived' with 'deleted'
Default to include archived — breaks every existing list view
Archive cascades to invites without warning — surprise consequence
No way to bulk archive — manual is painful at scale
