what_breaks_naive_approach: Add archivedAt, button calls PATCH. Forget DAL filter. List shows archived in main view. Users confused why their list grew. Or: cascade deletes that surprise users. Correct: explicit filter, badge in UI, undo within 30s.
reasoning: Adding a field is touching every layer. Start at DB with backfill plan (existing rows get archivedAt=null). DAL wrapper functions enforce default-exclude. Service has explicit archive/unarchive methods (not generic update). Search and export endpoints honor the same default filter. Frontend: list query param, toggle UI, distinct styling for archived rows. Undo toast for 30 seconds (write to soft-pending state, apply after timeout) reduces support tickets.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: CONFLICT if already archived/unarchived (idempotency)
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Toast error + restore optimistic state
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Project archived' with Undo
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: 30s Undo button; explicit Restore from archived view

edge_cases_addressed: Bulk archive — same op, transaction wraps
Archived project still appears in search — explicit includeArchived flag
Export ignores archived by default — same filter
Invitations to archived auto-cancelled
Mobile — same UX, button collapses to menu
Concurrent archive by two admins — second gets CONFLICT, handled silently
Restoring archived project — name conflict if another project took the slug
test_strategy: E2E: archive then list, gone. With showArchived=true, appears with badge. Undo within 30s, no server call. After 30s, server call confirmed. Restore works.
observability_hooks: archive funnel metrics. Most common undo reason (track separately).
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Touched ALL layers — junior misses one and ships a confusing UI. 2) Undo window deferred actual DB write — user trust dramatically improved. 3) Cancel related side effects (invitations) so archiving isn't a hidden surprise.

title: Concurrent edit detection with version field and conflict resolution UI
scenario_context: Two users edit same document simultaneously. Naive: last-write-wins, first user's changes lost silently. Need: optimistic concurrency control via version field; conflict UI lets second user merge/choose.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): version: Int field, incremented atomically on update
Layer 3 (Service): update accepts expectedVersion, throws ConflictError if mismatch
Layer 4 (Controller): 409 returns current version + current data so client can resolve
Layer 7 (State): client tracks version with data
Layer 8 (Component): on conflict, show diff UI with 'mine/theirs/merged' options
Layer 9 (Interaction): user resolves and retries with new version
Layer 10 (Observability): conflict_total{resolution_kind}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No version check — silent overwrites
Generic 'try again' on conflict — loses user's work
Long-lived locks — single user can block others
Field-level locking complexity for simple docs
what_breaks_naive_approach: Alice and Bob both open doc v3. Alice saves, doc becomes v4. Bob saves — overwrites Alice's changes silently. Alice's edits lost.
reasoning: Optimistic concurrency: version field; client sends expectedVersion; if DB version != expected, reject. Conflict response includes current state — client computes diff and renders three-pane UI. User picks side per field or merges. New save sends new expectedVersion. Performance: no locks, scales. Cost: occasional conflicts to resolve — UX matters.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: CONFLICT with current state in meta
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Conflict triggers resolver UI
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Another user saved changes' with diff view
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Merge/discard mine/keep mine

edge_cases_addressed: Both users save within 100ms — DB conditional update ensures one wins
Conflict on conflict (third user saves during resolution) — re-conflict, restart
Long resolution — version drifts further, still works
Field count grows — generic per-field resolver
Network drops during resolve — local state kept, retry
Read replica lag returns stale version — direct write to primary
Author identity shown — 'Bob saved 3s ago'
test_strategy: Concurrent integration test: two clients save same doc, first wins, second gets CONFLICT with current data. UI test: resolve flow.
observability_hooks: conflict_total tracked. High conflict rate per doc — UX problem (too many editors).
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Optimistic concurrency with version — no locks, scales. 2) Conflict response carries CURRENT state — client can resolve without another roundtrip. 3) Three-way UI (mine/theirs/merged) lets user keep their work; junior code loses it.

title: Admin impersonation with audit trail and visual indicator
scenario_context: Support needs to view users' accounts to debug. Naive: shared admin login with full access — no audit. Risk: insider snooping. Need: impersonation with audit log, clear UI banner, time-boxed sessions, scope limits.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): impersonation_sessions table; audit_log entries during session
Layer 3 (Service): impersonate(targetUserId, reason) checks admin perm, creates session, returns scoped token
Layer 4 (Controller): all subsequent requests include impersonatorId in audit context
Layer 5 (API contract): /me shows impersonated user; X-Impersonated-By header on responses
Layer 8 (Component): persistent banner: 'Acting as Bob (Support: Alice) — End session'
Layer 9 (Interaction): cannot impersonate restricted actions (billing changes, password reset)
Layer 10 (Observability): every action during impersonation logged with both actor + subject
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Shared admin login — no individual accountability
Impersonation without expiration — admin forgets, session lives forever
Banner that users can dismiss — accidentally hides risk
Logging only the action, not 'who actually performed it'
Allowing destructive actions in impersonation mode
what_breaks_naive_approach: Support agent impersonates user to debug. Forgets to log out. Hours later, user calls in, agent answers with stale account showing — confuses them. Or worse: insider impersonates VIP and exfiltrates data with no audit.
reasoning: Impersonation is sensitive — design it explicitly. Admin clicks 'Impersonate' on user record → modal asks reason (required text + dropdown). Server creates impersonation session (TTL 30min, max), returns scoped token marked as impersonation. Auth middleware sets req.user = target but req.actor = admin. Every audit log row carries actor + subject. UI shows persistent banner (sticky, not dismissable) reminding the admin. End-session button revokes token. Sensitive actions (delete account, change email, view billing source) blocked in impersonation mode.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: FORBIDDEN if not eligible to impersonate or for blocked actions
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Blocked actions show specific message: 'Not available during impersonation'
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Banner sticky; blocked-action explanations inline
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: End session button always visible

edge_cases_addressed: Token expires mid-session — auto-redirect with banner
Admin closes tab — server expires session after TTL
Tab in two windows — both show banner
Impersonation of admin — blocked (can't impersonate admins)
Cross-tenant impersonation — explicit scope check
Audit log query by sessionId returns full action timeline
Export audit log for compliance — CSV with all impersonation events
test_strategy: Integration: start impersonation → /me returns target. Sensitive action returns 403. End → token revoked. Audit log has start+actions+end with correct actor/subject.
observability_hooks: impersonation activity dashboard for security team. Alert on unusual patterns: long sessions, non-business hours, repeat subject.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Sticky non-dismissable banner — admins cannot forget they're impersonating. 2) Blocklist for sensitive actions — even with token, can't change auth-critical settings. 3) Audit log records ACTOR + SUBJECT — meeting SOC2/ISO requirements.

title: OpenAPI generated from Zod schemas with runtime + client SDK generation
scenario_context: Public API for a SaaS — external developers integrate. Docs drift from implementation. SDK in 3 languages — hand-written, out of sync. Need: Zod schemas → OpenAPI spec → docs → typed client SDKs, all from one source.
verification_chain: Layer 5 (API contract): all routes use Zod schemas; openapi spec generated in CI
Layer 5 (API contract): spec served at /openapi.json and rendered via Stoplight/Scalar at /docs
Layer 6 (Client API): TypeScript SDK auto-generated from spec; published on each version
Layer 10 (Observability): spec_drift_test in CI ensures runtime matches docs
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hand-written OpenAPI YAML — drifts
Examples in docs not validated against schema
Versioning by URL path AND header simultaneously — confusion
No deprecation strategy
what_breaks_naive_approach: Customer integrates against docs that say 'returns user_id'. Actual API returns 'userId'. Their integration breaks silently — null parsed where expected. With generated spec, types caught at compile time of customer's TS SDK.
reasoning: @asteasolutions/zod-to-openapi or zod-openapi convert Zod schemas to OpenAPI components. Routes registered with metadata: method, path, summary, request schema, response schemas per status. Spec served live. In CI: openapi-typescript or orval generates a TS client from the spec. Drift test compares generated SDK signatures against current routes — fails build if out of sync. Versioning via /v1/, /v2/ prefixes with shared schemas extending base types.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Spec includes error responses with codes/shapes
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Generated SDK throws typed errors
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: N/A — developer-facing
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Spec versioned; deprecation headers warn 6 months ahead

edge_cases_addressed: Schema with circular refs — registry uses $ref
Nullable vs optional — distinct in OpenAPI 3.1
Deprecated endpoints — marked in spec
Examples auto-generated from schemas
Breaking change → version bump via /v2/
API key vs OAuth — both schemes documented
Webhook payloads — separate spec section
test_strategy: Generate spec, validate against openapi linting rules. CI: generate SDK from spec, compare to checked-in SDK. Mock server from spec for client tests.
observability_hooks: api_version_usage to see when customers migrate. Endpoint-level usage for deprecation decisions.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Zod is the single source — no separate spec to drift. 2) SDK auto-generated, customers get typed clients. 3) Drift test in CI ensures runtime matches docs.

title: Outlier detection in time-series with quartile-based axis cropping
scenario_context: Latency chart shows p95 of API calls. Most data: 50-200ms. One spike to 30,000ms (timeout). Naive linear axis: everything looks flat at 0 with one tall spike. User can't see the daily pattern.
verification_chain: Layer 3 (Service): API returns raw values + quartiles
Layer 8 (Component): chart computes Tukey fences; offers log-scale toggle
Layer 8 (Component): outliers visually distinct (different color/marker, click for detail)
Layer 9 (Interaction): toggle 'crop outliers' vs 'show all'
Layer 10 (Observability): outlier_chart_toggle_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Auto-fit to outliers — useful pattern hidden
Hide outliers without telling user — pattern misleading
Hard-coded threshold — varies by data
Log scale by default — non-technical users confused
what_breaks_naive_approach: Engineering review meeting: 'why is the chart flat?' — wasting 5 min before someone realizes the spike. With outlier handling: chart shows daily pattern clearly + 'View 1 outlier' affordance.
reasoning: Tukey's fences: lower = Q1 - 1.5*IQR, upper = Q3 + 1.5*IQR. Points outside are outliers. Two presentation modes: (1) crop axis to fences, render outliers as floating markers at top edge with click-for-detail; (2) log scale showing all naturally. Default to mode 1 with toggle. Tooltip on cropped outlier shows true value. Always count outliers in summary.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Standard fetch errors
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Show 'unable to load' inline
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Outlier count visible; toggle for modes
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Retry

edge_cases_addressed: Few data points (< 4) — IQR meaningless, skip outlier handling
All values equal — IQR=0, no outliers possible
Negative values with log scale — fallback to linear
Zero values with log scale — start from 1
Single outlier dominates — capped at fence + 10%
Outlier count > 10 — banner suggests log scale
Reduced motion — no animations
test_strategy: Property test: random series, quartiles correct. Render with one extreme value — chart still readable. Storybook with various distributions.
observability_hooks: Track which mode users prefer per chart type.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Default to cropped — daily pattern visible, outliers still indicated. 2) Three modes give user control without overwhelming with options. 3) Outlier count surfaced — user knows data is being adjusted.

title: Metered billing: event collection, aggregation, and accurate invoice line items
scenario_context: API SaaS bills per 1k requests. Need: client SDK sends events, backend dedupes, aggregates per period, syncs to Stripe with reconciliation, presents accurate invoice preview to user.
verification_chain: Layer 1 (DB): usage_events with (tenantId, kind, occurredAt, idempotencyKey unique) index
Layer 3 (Service): batched aggregation job runs hourly, posts to Stripe meter
Layer 4 (Controller): /usage/preview returns current period's projected charge
Layer 5 (API contract): events ingested with idempotency key for client retries
Layer 6 (Client API): SDK batches events, retries on failure
Layer 10 (Observability): usage_event_dropped_total, stripe_sync_lag
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Sending one event per request to server — overhead
No idempotency — duplicates from retries
Aggregating on demand for invoice — slow, expensive
Showing usage with hour delay — user surprise on invoice
what_breaks_naive_approach: Client retries failed event → counted twice → user billed extra. Or: batch endpoint returns 500 after partial write → events lost. Idempotency keys + transactional batch insert fix both.
reasoning: Usage events: client batches in memory (max 100 or 30s), POSTs to /usage/events with idempotency keys per event. Server inserts with unique index on key (duplicates ignored). Hourly job aggregates events per tenant per kind, calls Stripe's usage records API. Preview endpoint reads recent events + posted records for live total. Reconciliation job daily compares posted records to source events, alerts on drift.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Validation errors per event in batch; partial success returned
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Failed batch re-queued; persistent failure → events lost (acceptable for metering)
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Preview includes 'may differ slightly' disclaimer
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: User can refresh; reconciliation job catches drift

edge_cases_addressed: Client offline — queue persists in memory; flush on online
Clock skew — events with future timestamps clamped to now
Spike (10k events/sec) — server batch insert handles
Timezone for billing period — UTC enforced
Stripe rate limit — backoff per tenant
Reconciliation finds drift — emit alert, no auto-correct
Plan downgrade mid-period — proration computed correctly
test_strategy: Property: 1000 events with duplicate keys → counted once. Stripe sandbox: full sync, assert quantities match. Drift: inject discrepancy, reconciliation detects.
observability_hooks: Per-tenant usage gauge, per-kind event rate, Stripe sync lag dashboard. Alert: lag > 6h.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Idempotency at ingest — client retries safe by design. 2) Preview + final invoice align — no billing surprises. 3) Reconciliation catches drift — Stripe API failures don't silently lose revenue.

title: Tremor dashboard with React Query, real-time refresh, and CSV export
scenario_context: Internal ops dashboard built fast with Tremor. Cards show key metrics. Need: auto-refresh, manual refresh button, CSV export per card, loading skeletons matching Tremor's card layout.
verification_chain: Layer 6 (Client API): React Query for caching + refetching
Layer 7 (State): per-card query state
Layer 8 (Component): Tremor cards with custom loading skeletons
Layer 9 (Interaction): refresh button + auto every 60s; export to CSV
Layer 10 (Observability): card_load_duration per card
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Refetching all cards simultaneously every 60s — request burst
CSV export client-side from displayed data — incomplete (only loaded rows)
Skeleton mismatched with card height — CLS
Refresh button without disabled state — double-clicks fire two requests
what_breaks_naive_approach: 15 cards, 15 fetches every minute — server load spikes. Stagger by index, use cache. Or: 'Export CSV' downloads only visible rows — exec wonders where data is. Use server-side export endpoint.
reasoning: React Query with staleTime and refetchInterval per card. Stagger refreshes by index*2s. Tremor's Card + Metric + AreaChart for visuals. Skeletons mirror dimensions. Export endpoints return CSVs from server (full data). Refresh button uses isFetching to disable.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Per-card 5xx → card-level error with retry
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: isError shows per-card retry

title: Deprecate API field with telemetry-tracked usage before removal
scenario_context: Old API field `legacy_user_name` superseded by `fullName`. Need to remove. Don't know who's still using it. Need: deprecation header, response includes both, telemetry counts which field is read, removal only after usage drops to zero.
verification_chain: Layer 5 (API contract): response includes both fields; OpenAPI marks legacy deprecated
Layer 5 (API contract): response Deprecation header with sunset date
Layer 6 (Client API): client wrappers read new field, log if accessing legacy via Proxy
Layer 10 (Observability): legacy_field_access_total{field, caller}
Process: rollout — telemetry — sunset email — removal
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Removing fields without notice — integration breakage
Long-term keeping both — codebase rot
No telemetry — guess when safe
Deprecation only in docs — devs don't read
what_breaks_naive_approach: Remove field. Integration partners' apps crash. Support flood. With telemetry, you remove only after usage at zero for 30 days; partners notified in advance.
reasoning: Multi-step: (1) add new field, keep old; (2) instrument old field read via Proxy (server-side) or deprecation header tracking via clients; (3) send deprecation notices to API consumers (X-Deprecation, email); (4) monitor metric until zero for N days; (5) remove. Server can detect legacy reads via a wrapper around the serializer. Clients can wrap responses in a Proxy that logs property access — opt-in for first-party clients.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: N/A — field still works
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Soft warning in dev, telemetry in prod
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Banner on dev portal for affected accounts
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Switch to new field per migration guide

edge_cases_addressed: Internal callers — instrumented + fixed first
External callers — emailed with usage data
Caching layer hides reads — log at cache miss too
SDK auto-uses new field — old field reads drop sharply
Browser apps cache — set short max-age on deprecation
Versioned API — old version preserves field longer
Removal date passed but usage non-zero — re-evaluate
test_strategy: Integration: legacy_user_name appears + logged. After removal: 404 with helpful error code.
observability_hooks: Daily dashboard: deprecated_field_reads. Email digest to affected customers. Removal gate: 30 days at zero.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Telemetry-driven removal — no guesses. 2) Both server and client instrumented — catches both first and third party usage. 3) Migration guide linked in headers — devs find it from response inspection.

title: Auth token expiry mid-request with refresh + queued retry
scenario_context: User has active session, token expires while form open. They submit, get 401. Naive: dump them to login, losing their input. Need: silent refresh, request retry, queue concurrent requests during refresh.
verification_chain: Layer 5 (API contract): 401 with code TOKEN_EXPIRED distinguishes from invalid token
Layer 6 (Client API): interceptor catches 401, refreshes token, retries original request
Layer 6 (Client API): concurrent 401s share one refresh attempt
Layer 7 (State): auth state updated post-refresh
Layer 9 (Interaction): user never sees the refresh
Layer 10 (Observability): token_refresh_success/fail; mean_session_length
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Redirect to login on any 401 — loses unsaved work
Refresh on every request — wastes calls
No mutex on refresh — N concurrent requests trigger N refreshes
Storing refresh token in localStorage — XSS exposure
what_breaks_naive_approach: User typed long form, token expired, click submit, get bounced to login, form gone. With refresh: token silently renewed, save succeeds.
reasoning: Access token in memory with short TTL (5-15 min); refresh token in HttpOnly cookie. Fetch interceptor: on 401 with TOKEN_EXPIRED, call refresh. Single in-flight refresh promise — concurrent requests await it, then retry. Failed refresh → real logout. Refresh endpoint validates refresh token, rotates it (revoke old), returns new access token.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: 401 with code TOKEN_EXPIRED for expiry vs UNAUTHORIZED for invalid
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: TOKEN_EXPIRED → silent refresh + retry; UNAUTHORIZED → logout
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: None on successful refresh; logout shows reason
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Login with returnTo param preserves destination

edge_cases_addressed: 10 concurrent fetches all 401 — one refresh, all retry with new token
Refresh token also expired — logout with form preservation hint
Refresh succeeds but original request body was consumed — clone before retry
User in two tabs — refresh in one updates cookie, other tab gets new token on next 401
Server clock skew — short TTL minimizes impact
HTTPS-only cookie — secure flag set
XSRF on refresh — sameSite strict + custom header
test_strategy: Mock 401 then 200 after refresh. Assert one refresh call for 10 concurrent. Refresh failure → redirect with returnTo.
observability_hooks: token_refresh metrics; session_length distribution. Alert on refresh failure spike.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Single in-flight refresh promise — N concurrent 401s share one refresh. 2) Distinguish TOKEN_EXPIRED from UNAUTHORIZED — only one should trigger silent recovery. 3) Refresh token in HttpOnly cookie — XSS can't steal it; access token in memory only.
